lines.raw_text: **AI-driven adaptive acoustic communications using software-defined radio (SDR) stands out as the most energy-efficient method for transmitting data between two underwater points, by performing local data processing and dynamically optimizing transmission parameters.**

spans.text: **AI-driven adaptive acoustic communications using software-defined radio (SDR)
stands out as the most energy-efficient method
for transmitting data between two underwater points, by performing local data processing and dynamically optimizing transmission parameters.**

lines.raw_text: ### Why This Approach Excels
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Why This Approach Excels</null>

lines.raw_text: Traditional underwater communication faces severe challenges: radio waves attenuate rapidly in water, limiting range; optical methods (like VLC) work short distances but struggle across air-water boundaries or deeper waters; acoustic waves dominate due to better propagation but suffer low bandwidth, high latency, and high energy use for raw data transmission[1][3].

spans.text: Traditional underwater communication faces severe challenges: radio waves attenuate rapidly in water, limiting range; optical methods (like VLC) work short distances but struggle across air-water boundaries or deeper waters; acoustic waves dominate due to better propagation but suffer low bandwidth, high latency, and high energy use for raw data transmission[1][3].

lines.raw_text: The leading solution from recent research integrates **lightweight AI models** (e.g., CNNs) directly on underwater sensor nodes for tasks like marine species classification. Instead of sending raw sensor data—which consumes massive energy—the nodes transmit only compact results (e.g., classifications), slashing data volume[1].

lines.tagged_text: <null>### Key Energy-Saving Mechanisms</null>

lines.raw_text: - **Localized Inference**: AI processes data onboard, reducing transmission needs by up to orders of magnitude[1].

lines.raw_text: - **Adaptive Transmission via SDR**: Dynamically adjusts **modulation schemes**, **packet length**, and **power** based on data semantics, channel conditions, and environmental impact. GNU Radio simulations confirm lower energy use, bit error rate (BER), and delay compared to fixed-parameter systems[1].

lines.tagged_text: <null>### Practical Considerations</null>

lines.raw_text: This AI-SDR framework is simulated and validated for real-time deployment on SDR platforms like GNU Radio, making it scalable for battery-constrained nodes in wireless underwater sensor networks (UWSNs)[1]. For pure point-to-point (no air interface), it outperforms alternatives by addressing the intertwined issues of reliability and energy[3].

lines.raw_text: ### Thruster and Propulsion Optimization
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Thruster and Propulsion Optimization</null>

lines.raw_text: Improving thruster configuration is one of the most direct ways to boost **ROV maneuverability**. Vectoring or tilting thrusters allows dynamic adjustment of the forward vector for intricate trajectories and better adaptation to complex environments, though larger tilt angles trade off forward efficiency for ascent speed[1]. Adding **lateral thrusters** enhances responsiveness in currents, combined with auto-heading features that automatically maintain or adjust course using compass feedback[4]. Innovations like integrated rotators can offset the ROV's heading relative to the surface vessel, saving power and improving precision in tasks like rock installation[5].

lines.raw_text: ### Hull and Drag Reduction
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Hull and Drag Reduction</null>

lines.raw_text: Streamline your ROV's design to minimize drag, which directly enhances agility. Use **lightweight, corrosion-resistant materials** like aluminum, composites, or titanium to improve buoyancy and reduce overall mass without sacrificing strength[8]. In strong currents, attach a **clump weight** to the tether (e.g., via carabiner over 6+ inches of tether) to stabilize it and counter drag, allowing the ROV to "kite" upstream more predictably[4]. For hull-cleaning ROVs, switch to **magnetic wheels/tracks or vacuum clamps** for crawling instead of constant thruster use, reducing power draw and enabling stable maneuvering on curved surfaces[3].

lines.raw_text: ### Sensors and Situational Awareness
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Sensors and Situational Awareness</null>

lines.raw_text: Enhanced sensing prevents collisions and eases piloting. Equip with **wide-field-of-view (FOV) cameras** like the Discovery Vision System for better obstacle detection, tether monitoring, and reduced repositioning needs, lowering pilot workload[2]. Add **sonar** for low-visibility navigation, hazard previewing, and SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) to build real-time 3D maps and enable autonomous path planning (e.g., A* or RRT* algorithms)[3][4][7]. **AI vision lock** can stabilize the ROV on targets automatically[5].

lines.raw_text: ### Control Systems and Autonomy
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Control Systems and Autonomy</null>

lines.raw_text: Upgrade software for smarter operation. Implement **auto-heading and dynamic positioning (DP)** with multicore processors to handle complex loops efficiently, improving control in confined spaces[4][5]. For custom builds, add degrees of freedom in pitch/roll via buoyancy adjustments or legged mechanisms to traverse rugged terrain[1]. Semi-autonomous features like path optimization reduce manual input while maximizing coverage[3].

lines.raw_text: **Underwater wireless optical communication (UWOC) modems transmit data at high speeds (tens of Mbps) using visible light—typically blue-green wavelengths (400-500 nm)—instead of acoustics or radio waves, enabling low-latency links over short to medium distances in water.[1][3][4][5]**

)—instead of acoustics or radio waves, enabling low-latency links over short to medium distances in water.[1][3][4][5]**

lines.raw_text: They overcome water's absorption and scattering of signals by employing focused laser diodes (LDs) or LEDs as light sources, modulation techniques like OFDM or QPSK, and line-of-sight (LOS) configurations for reliable, secure communication.[1][3][6]

lines.raw_text: ### Key Components
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Key Components</null>

lines.raw_text: UWOC modems consist of a **transmitter**, **channel**, and **receiver**:
lines.tagged_text: <null>UWOC modems consist of a **transmitter**, **channel**, and **receiver**:</null>

spans.text: UWOC modems consist of a **transmitter**, **channel**, and **receiver**:

lines.raw_text: - **Transmitter**: Encodes electrical data via a modulator (e.g., OFDM, QAM, or MSM for multi-receiver scenarios), drives an LED or LD to emit pulsed light, and uses lenses for beam focusing in point-to-point LOS or diffuse LOS setups.[1][4]
lines.tagged_text: <null>- **Transmitter**: Encodes electrical data via a modulator (e.g., OFDM, QAM, or MSM for multi-receiver scenarios), drives an LED or LD to emit pulsed light, and uses lenses for beam focusing in point-to-point LOS or diffuse LOS setups.[1][4]</null>

spans.text: - **Transmitter**: Encodes electrical data via a modulator (e.g., OFDM, QAM, or MSM for multi-receiver scenarios), drives an LED or LD to emit pulsed light, and uses lenses for beam focusing in point-to-point LOS or diffuse LOS setups.[1][4]

lines.raw_text: - **Channel**: Pure seawater, clear ocean, coastal, or turbid harbor water; blue-green light minimizes attenuation (low absorption/scattering), but range is limited (e.g., tens of meters) by particles, turbidity, and background noise like sunlight.[1][4][5]

lines.raw_text: - **Receiver**: Collects light with lenses, converts it to photocurrent via avalanche photodiodes (APDs), amplifies with trans-impedance amps, digitizes signals, and decodes data while rejecting noise.[1][4]

lines.raw_text: ### How Data Transmission Works
lines.tagged_text: <null>### How Data Transmission Works</null>

lines.raw_text: 1. Electrical data modulates light intensity or phase at the transmitter, creating optical pulses.[1][4]
lines.tagged_text: <null>1. Electrical data modulates light intensity or phase at the transmitter, creating optical pulses.[1][4]</null>

spans.text: 1. Electrical data modulates light intensity or phase at the transmitter, creating optical pulses.[1][4]

lines.raw_text: 2. Focused beams propagate through water; narrow beams (e.g., eye-safe lasers) enable long-distance links by concentrating energy and rejecting interference.[5]
lines.tagged_text: <null>2. Focused beams propagate through water; narrow beams (e.g., eye-safe lasers) enable long-distance links by concentrating energy and rejecting interference.[5]</null>

spans.text: 2. Focused beams propagate through water; narrow beams (e.g., eye-safe lasers) enable long-distance links by concentrating energy and rejecting interference.[5]

lines.raw_text: 3. Receiver detects photons, converts to electrical signals, and demodulates to recover data—achieving full-duplex (bi-directional) at 10s-500 Mbps.[3][6]
lines.tagged_text: <null>3. Receiver detects photons, converts to electrical signals, and demodulates to recover data—achieving full-duplex (bi-directional) at </null><StatisticalUse>10s-500 Mbps</StatisticalUse><null>.[3][6]</null>

spans.text: 3. Receiver detects photons, converts to electrical signals, and demodulates to recover data—achieving full-duplex (bi-directional) at

lines.raw_text: 4. Alignment is critical: 3° misalignment can reduce signal by 100x in clear water; hybrid opto-acoustic modems extend range for longer distances.[2][4]

lines.raw_text: **Use untethered or minimally tethered inspection tools like autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs), battery-powered drone crawlers, snake robots, or borescope cameras to avoid tangling in confined submerged spaces.** These technologies enable remote visual and sensor-based inspections without the risks of long tethers snagging on debris, joints, or irregular surfaces.[1][2][4][5][6]

lines.raw_text: ### Recommended Technologies for Tangle-Free Submerged Inspections
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Recommended Technologies for Tangle-Free Submerged Inspections</null>

spans.text: ### Recommended Technologies for Tangle-Free Submerged Inspections

lines.raw_text: - **Remotely Operated Vehicles (ROVs) with Short or Managed Tethers**: In long-distance submerged tunnels, compact ROVs like the Deep Trekker Pivot use high-resolution cameras and sonar for detailed assessments of walls, joints, debris, and infrastructure. They minimize crew needs and work in low visibility, but tether management is key—opt for models designed for confined navigation to reduce snags.[1]
lines.tagged_text: <null>- **Remotely Operated Vehicles (ROVs) with Short or Managed Tethers**: In long-distance submerged tunnels, compact ROVs like the </null><BrandPositioning>Deep Trekker Pivot</BrandPositioning><null> use high-resolution cameras and sonar for detailed assessments of walls, joints, debris, and infrastructure. They minimize crew needs and work in low visibility, but tether management is key—opt for models designed for confined navigation to reduce snags.[1]</null>

lines.raw_text: - **Wireless or Short-Range Robotic Crawlers and Snakes**: Crawler robots, snake robots, and magnetic climber robots access pipes, ducts, and submerged metallic surfaces without long tethers. These provide remote visual inspection (RVI) and non-destructive testing (NDT) like ultrasonic or eddy current, eliminating human entry and tangling risks.[2][4][5]

lines.raw_text: - **Borescope and Push Cameras**: Flexible, probe-based cameras (up to 400 feet long) with explosion-proof options for hazardous submerged areas allow video recording and image capture through small entry points. They reduce confined space entry permits and costs while avoiding tether issues entirely.[6]

lines.raw_text: **Yes, hubless thrusters (also called rim-driven or hubless propeller thrusters) are commercially available and designed specifically for underwater vehicles like ROVs, AUVs, and subsea drones.**[1][2][3][4][5]
lines.tagged_text: <null>**Yes, hubless thrusters (also called rim-driven or hubless propeller thrusters) are commercially available and designed specifically for underwater vehicles like ROVs, AUVs, and subsea drones.**[1][2][3][4][5]</null>

spans.text: **Yes, hubless thrusters (also called rim-driven or hubless propeller thrusters) are commercially available and designed specifically for underwater vehicles like ROVs, AUVs, and subsea drones.**[1][2][3][4][5]

lines.raw_text: These thrusters feature a **rim-driven design** where the propeller is powered by an outer rim motor rather than a central hub, eliminating the hub to reduce drag, prevent entanglement in debris (e.g., ropes, seaweed, or plants), and enable oil-free, pressure-proof operation.[1][2][3][5] Key benefits include low noise for marine life protection, high reliability with seawater-lubricated bearings, and quick propeller swaps without tools.[1][2][7]

lines.raw_text: ### Performance and Applications
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Performance and Applications</null>

lines.raw_text: These thrusters excel in cluttered environments, reducing mission failures from entanglements, as seen in USV surveys where traditional props failed.[3] They're used for reef monitoring, pipeline inspections, and deep-sea exploration, often cutting costs by up to 75% compared to manned dives.[4] A pilot study also explores scaling hubless rim-driven thrusters for larger inland vessels.[6]

lines.raw_text: ### Recommended Propeller Options
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Recommended Propeller Options</null>

lines.raw_text: These solutions physically shield or enclose propellers while maintaining performance:
lines.tagged_text: <null>These solutions physically shield or enclose propellers while maintaining performance:</null>

spans.text: These solutions physically shield or enclose propellers while maintaining performance:

lines.raw_text: - **Propeller Protectors/Guards**: Snap-on guards (e.g., 6-per-set for FIFISH ROVs) block grass, rocks, seaweed, and other debris. They use lightweight, rugged, eco-friendly materials for easy installation without sacrificing speed, power, or control.[2]

spans.text: ) block grass, rocks, seaweed, and other debris. They use lightweight, rugged, eco-friendly materials for easy installation without sacrificing speed, power, or control.[2]

lines.raw_text: - **Shrouded Propellers**: Enclosed designs paired with brushless DC motors provide efficient thrust and minimize entanglement risks in cluttered waters.[6]

lines.raw_text: - **Hardware Modifications**: Trident ROV users apply specific mods (e.g., guards or adjusted blades) to avoid jamming in seagrasses, with piloting guides available for debris navigation.[4]

lines.raw_text: **Smaller subsea thrusters, particularly those with high RPM electric motors and appropriately sized propellers (e.g., 6.5-9.5 inches), generally offer the best thrust-to-volume ratio for ROVs due to their compact size, reduced gearing needs, and efficient scaling.** [1][2]

lines.raw_text: ### Key Factors Influencing Thrust-to-Volume Ratio
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Key Factors Influencing Thrust-to-Volume Ratio</null>

lines.raw_text: Thrust-to-volume ratio prioritizes high thrust output relative to the thruster's physical size (diameter, length, and overall envelope), which is critical for compact ROV designs where space is limited.
lines.tagged_text: <null>Thrust-to-volume ratio prioritizes high thrust output relative to the thruster's physical size (diameter, length, and overall envelope), which is critical for compact ROV designs where space is limited.</null>

spans.text: Thrust-to-volume ratio prioritizes high thrust output relative to the thruster's physical size (diameter, length, and overall envelope), which is critical for compact ROV designs where space is limited.

lines.raw_text: - **Smaller thrusters excel in efficiency scaling**: Smaller units achieve comparable prop tip speeds to larger ones at higher RPMs, minimizing motor weight and avoiding heavy gearing. This results in better thrust per unit volume, though larger thrusters gain slight efficiency from lower skin friction (higher Reynolds number).[2]
lines.tagged_text: <null>- **Smaller thrusters excel in efficiency scaling**: Smaller units achieve comparable prop tip speeds to larger ones at higher RPMs, minimizing motor weight and avoiding heavy gearing. This results in better thrust per unit volume, though larger thrusters gain slight efficiency from lower skin friction (higher Reynolds number).[2]</null>

spans.text: - **Smaller thrusters excel in efficiency scaling**: Smaller units achieve comparable prop tip speeds to larger ones at higher RPMs, minimizing motor weight and avoiding heavy gearing. This results in better thrust per unit volume, though larger thrusters gain slight efficiency from lower skin friction (higher Reynolds number).[2]

lines.raw_text: - **Propeller pitch and configuration matter**: Low-pitch propellers (pitch ≤ half diameter) deliver high static thrust with low power, ideal for ROV maneuvering in tight spaces. Propeller-based setups outperform impeller/pump designs (e.g., bilge pumps) in watts per pound of thrust, with reverse thrust capability adding versatility.[1][4]

lines.raw_text: **Yes, several underwater robots are designed to navigate complex piping systems, particularly for inspection, mapping, and maintenance in water pipes and pipelines.** These robots address challenges like rust, calcite buildup, pipe irregularities, and flow, using advanced propulsion, sensors, and control systems.[1][2]

lines.raw_text: ### Key Examples and Capabilities
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Key Examples and Capabilities</null>

lines.raw_text: - **MIT Pipe Inspection Robots**: Autonomous underwater robots developed since 2008 for water pipe networks. They feature specialized subsystems for propulsion, braking, and turning to handle confined spaces with rust and deposits. Prototypes enable efficient navigation for mapping and inspection in liquid-filled pipes.[1]

lines.raw_text: - **BlueROV2-Based In-Pipe System**: A low-cost setup using sonar (Ping1D and Ping360) for centering and traversal in submerged 46 cm-diameter pipes. It autonomously handles ambient flow, deformations, and irregularities via geometric modeling and PD controllers, without needing Doppler logs or external tracking.[2]

lines.raw_text: - **Location and Focus**: A Swiss deeptech startup developing modular, high-performance wireless underwater communication systems using ultrasonic waves for long-range, high-data-rate transmission in challenging environments. It supports applications like UUVs, divers, sensors, submarines, and search-and-rescue operations.[1][2][4]

lines.raw_text: ## About Tethys Robotics
lines.tagged_text: <null>## About </null><BrandPositioning>Tethys Robotics</BrandPositioning>

lines.raw_text: Tethys Robotics develops the **Tethys ONE**, a hybrid underwater drone designed for remote offshore inspections[3]. The company is based in Zurich and operates within Switzerland's robotics ecosystem[7]. Their drones are specifically engineered for challenging underwater environments and can operate autonomously in conditions where traditional methods are difficult or unsafe[2].

lines.raw_text: Yes, there are **optical wireless modems designed to work in turbid water**. Several companies have developed systems specifically engineered to handle challenging water conditions.

lines.raw_text: **Hydromea's LUMA X** is one of the most advanced options available[3]. This modem can transmit data at up to 10 Mbit/s with a 120-degree cone and is sensitive enough to work without requiring direct line of sight in murky waters[3]. It features patent-pending technology to operate in various lighting conditions and turbidity levels, with pressure ratings up to 6,000 meters[3].

lines.raw_text: **Shimadzu Corporation** has also developed an optical/acoustic hybrid underwater communication modem that intelligently switches between optical communication for short and medium distances and acoustic communication for scenarios where turbidity and ambient light are strong[2].

lines.raw_text: The key challenge with optical underwater communication is that water turbidity causes signal dispersion and attenuation[1]. However, modern designs use semiconductor lasers with superior directivity and sensitive receivers to overcome these limitations in various water conditions. The practical transmission distances depend on water clarity—research shows acceptable performance up to 30 meters in clear water, 15 meters in coastal waters, and 6 meters in harbor waters with high turbidity[1].

lines.raw_text: Yes, there are underwater thrusters with integrated controllers that eliminate the need for external housings. The **TDM7 Pro** is a prime example, featuring an **integrated ESC (Electronic Speed Controller)** that streamlines setup and eliminates the need for additional external components and wiring[4]. This built-in controller is sealed against water, dust, sand, and corrosion, allowing the thruster to operate at depths up to 100 meters[4].

lines.raw_text: The advantage of integrated controllers is significant: they reduce complexity by combining the motor and speed controller into a single unit, which simplifies installation and reduces the overall footprint compared to traditional setups where the ESC is housed separately. This integrated approach is particularly useful for space-constrained underwater applications like ROVs (remotely operated vehicles) and autonomous underwater vehicles[4].

lines.raw_text: ## Proven ROV Systems
lines.tagged_text: <null>## Proven ROV Systems</null>

lines.raw_text: **EXRAY™ ROV** – This system is purpose-built for ballast tank inspections and has demonstrated significant efficiency gains[2]. It features wireless underwater inspection capabilities with proprietary LUMA™ wireless communication technology for real-time data streaming and high-resolution imaging[5]. The EXRAY™ has extended dive time capabilities and integrates with the NAVIA™ data management system for automatic reporting[2].

lines.raw_text: **Top-rated portable ROVs for rapid deployment in search and rescue (SAR) include the Deep Trekker PIVOT, Deep Trekker DTG3, Oceanbotics SRV-8, and FIFISH series, praised for quick setup (under 3 minutes), stability in currents, high-res cameras, and integration with sonar or positioning systems.** These models stand out in SAR applications due to their compactness, ease of use by first responders, and proven performance in low-visibility or hazardous waters like rivers and lakes.[1][2][3][4]

lines.raw_text: ### Key Details on UWOC
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Key Details on UWOC</null>

lines.raw_text: - **High Bandwidth Advantage**: Visible light offers a large bandwidth and low absorption in water compared to acoustics, making it ideal for transmitting large volumes of data like high-resolution images, video, and sensor feeds in real-time.[2][3]
lines.tagged_text: <null>- **High Bandwidth Advantage**: Visible light offers a large bandwidth and low absorption in water compared to acoustics, </null><StrongRecommendation>making it ideal for transmitting large volumes of data like high-resolution images, video, and sensor feeds in real-time</StrongRecommendation><null>.[2][3]</null>

lines.raw_text: - **Short-Range Suitability**: Effective over distances of a few meters to tens of meters, limited by light scattering and absorption in water; for example, Kyocera's system targets short-range applications in ocean exploration and underwater robotics.[3]

lines.raw_text: **Wireless underwater control of subsea valves primarily requires acoustic modems, optical wireless modems, or magnetic induction systems for communication, paired with subsea valve actuators, sensors, and control interfaces.** These setups enable remote operation without physical cables, replacing traditional hydraulics for faster, more flexible control in oil & gas, renewables, and subsea operations.[2][4][5]

lines.raw_text: Underwater robots primarily communicate in real-time using **acoustic signals** for long-range, low-bandwidth needs and **optical (light-based) modems** for short-range, high-bandwidth applications, with emerging hybrids and alternatives like motion gestures or magnetic fields.[1][3][5]

lines.raw_text: ### Key Communication Methods
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Key Communication Methods</null>

lines.raw_text: - **Acoustic Communication**: Relies on sound waves for reliable wireless links over kilometers, suitable for navigation updates or basic coordination in swarms, but limited to low data rates (not ideal for video).[3][5]
lines.tagged_text: <null>- **Acoustic Communication**: Relies on sound waves for reliable wireless links over kilometers, suitable for navigation updates or basic coordination in swarms, but limited to low data rates (not ideal for video).[3][5]</null>

spans.text: - **Acoustic Communication**: Relies on sound waves for reliable wireless links over kilometers, suitable for navigation updates or basic coordination in swarms, but limited to low data rates (not ideal for video).[3][5]

lines.raw_text: - **Optical Communication**: Uses blue-green light for megabit-per-second speeds and millisecond latency over tens to hundreds of meters in clear water, enabling real-time control, video streaming, or multi-robot operation without tethers. Performance drops in turbid conditions.[1][4][5]

lines.raw_text: ### Challenges and Advances
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Challenges and Advances</null>

lines.raw_text: Real-time performance requires low latency (e.g., 1.48 ms packet times in optical systems) and error correction like CRC checks or FPGA decoders.[1] Swarming demands scalable navigation/command schemes, with optics/high-frequency acoustics supporting close-proximity groups.[4] Limitations include water turbidity for optics and narrow bandwidth for acoustics; ongoing work explores AI-driven coordination and surface-penetrating links.[3][4]

lines.raw_text: ### Key Strategies to Reduce Costs in Offshore Oil and Gas Structural Inspections
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Key Strategies to Reduce Costs in Offshore Oil and Gas Structural Inspections</null>

spans.text: ### Key Strategies to Reduce Costs in Offshore Oil and Gas Structural Inspections

lines.raw_text: Adopting advanced technologies like integrated inspection systems, drones (UAVs), risk-based inspection (RBI) methods, and autonomous vehicles can significantly cut costs by streamlining workflows, minimizing human intervention, and optimizing inspection frequency.[1][3][4][6]
lines.tagged_text: <null>Adopting advanced technologies like integrated inspection systems, drones (UAVs), risk-based inspection (RBI) methods, and autonomous vehicles can significantly cut costs by streamlining workflows, minimizing human intervention, and optimizing inspection frequency.[1][3][4][6]</null>

spans.text: Adopting advanced technologies like integrated inspection systems, drones (UAVs), risk-based inspection (RBI) methods, and autonomous vehicles can significantly cut costs by streamlining workflows, minimizing human intervention, and optimizing inspection frequency.[1][3][4][6]

lines.raw_text: #### 1. **Integrate Systems for Streamlined Workflows**
lines.tagged_text: <null>#### 1. **Integrate Systems for Streamlined Workflows**</null>

lines.raw_text: - Use single-interface systems like the Rayfin Single Channel Inspection (SCI) that combine cameras, lights, and lasers, reducing software switching, labor time, and training needs. This eliminates redundancy and ensures compatibility with existing ROVs or tow systems, leading to faster inspections and lower operational expenses.[1]
lines.tagged_text: <null>   - Use single-interface systems like the </null><BrandPositioning>Rayfin Single Channel Inspection (SCI)</BrandPositioning><null> that combine cameras, lights, and lasers, reducing software switching, labor time, and training needs. This eliminates redundancy and ensures compatibility with existing ROVs or tow systems, leading to faster inspections and lower operational expenses.[1]</null>

spans.text: that combine cameras, lights, and lasers, reducing software switching, labor time, and training needs. This eliminates redundancy and ensures compatibility with existing ROVs or tow systems, leading to faster inspections and lower operational expenses.[1]

lines.raw_text: - Seamless integration minimizes additional equipment purchases and operator training costs.[1]
lines.tagged_text: <null>   - Seamless integration minimizes additional equipment purchases and operator training costs.[1]</null>

spans.text: - Seamless integration minimizes additional equipment purchases and operator training costs.[1]

lines.raw_text: #### 2. **Leverage Drones and Autonomous Vehicles (UAVs/AUVs)**
lines.tagged_text: <null>#### 2. **Leverage Drones and Autonomous Vehicles (UAVs/AUVs)**</null>

lines.raw_text: - Deploy next-generation **UAVs** equipped with electromagnets for ultrasonic testing, 3D laser scanning (to 2mm accuracy), and live HD video streaming. These enable non-destructive testing from inaccessible areas, longer flight times, real-time data to onshore teams (reducing offshore personnel), and instant results—cutting team sizes and reporting time.[3][6]
lines.tagged_text: <null>   - Deploy next-generation **UAVs** equipped with electromagnets for ultrasonic testing, 3D laser scanning (to </null><StatisticalUse>2mm accuracy</StatisticalUse><null>), and live HD video streaming. These enable non-destructive testing from inaccessible areas, longer flight times, real-time data to onshore teams (reducing offshore personnel), and instant results—cutting team sizes and reporting time.[3][6]</null>

spans.text: - Deploy next-generation **UAVs** equipped with electromagnets for ultrasonic testing, 3D laser scanning (to

), and live HD video streaming. These enable non-destructive testing from inaccessible areas, longer flight times, real-time data to onshore teams (reducing offshore personnel), and instant results—cutting team sizes and reporting time.[3][6]

lines.raw_text: - Use **AUVs** for underwater pipeline and structural inspections to avoid costly diver support vessels (DSVs, which cost orders of magnitude more than ROV support vessels).[4][6]
lines.tagged_text: <null>   - Use **AUVs** for underwater pipeline and structural inspections to avoid costly diver support vessels (DSVs, which cost orders of magnitude more than ROV support vessels).[4][6]</null>

spans.text: - Use **AUVs** for underwater pipeline and structural inspections to avoid costly diver support vessels (DSVs, which cost orders of magnitude more than ROV support vessels).[4][6]

lines.raw_text: #### 3. **Implement Risk-Based and Probabilistic Inspection Planning**
lines.tagged_text: <null>#### 3. **Implement Risk-Based and Probabilistic Inspection Planning**</null>

lines.raw_text: - Apply **probabilistic RBI** using Monte Carlo simulations and Bayesian inference to predict fatigue risks from inspections and monitoring data (e.g., accelerometers). This shifts from annual subsea inspections to every 3-4 years for ageing jackets, optimizing resource allocation while keeping failure risks low—potentially saving substantial vessel hire costs.[4]

These are low-cost compared to invasive methods like saturation diving and enable predictive maintenance, extending asset life and reducing servicing frequency.[4][6]

lines.raw_text: **Acoustic modems provide the most reliable and versatile method for transferring data underwater without tethered cables, offering long-range communication suitable for most applications despite limitations like noise susceptibility.** [1][3][4]

lines.tagged_text: <null>### Practical Considerations and Examples</null>

lines.raw_text: - **For long-range/deep water** (e.g., 500-2000m): Acoustics via surface relay (buoy to radio) is common; real-time low-rate video (~1kbps) is feasible but challenging under £1000 with battery power.[4]

lines.raw_text: **No single drone is universally the "best" for inspecting potable water reservoirs, as the optimal choice depends on whether you're targeting **exterior structural checks** (e.g., tanks, coatings, hatches) or **interior/submerged inspections** (e.g., sediment, corrosion), along with factors like environment (wooded/remote areas, wind, moisture) and regulations.**

spans.text: **No single drone is universally the "best" for inspecting potable water reservoirs, as the optimal choice depends on whether you're targeting **exterior structural checks** (e.g., tanks, coatings, hatches) or **interior/submerged inspections** (e.g., sediment, corrosion), along with factors like environment (wooded/remote areas, wind, moisture) and regulations.**

lines.raw_text: DJI enterprise models like the **Matrice 350 RTK** or **Matrice 400 RTK** stand out for most exterior potable water reservoir inspections due to their long flight times (up to 55 minutes), modular payloads (zoom, thermal for detecting leaks or defects), RTK precision for accurate mapping, and robustness in challenging conditions.[2][4][5][6]

lines.raw_text: ### Why These Excel for Potable Water Reservoirs
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Why These Excel for Potable Water Reservoirs</null>

spans.text: ### Why These Excel for Potable Water Reservoirs

lines.raw_text: Potable water inspections prioritize **safety** (no climbing/diving), **high-res detail** for defects like cracks/seals, and **efficiency** in hard-to-reach spots (e.g., steep faces, fluctuating levels).[1][3][7] Drones reduce time/cost vs. traditional methods (scaffolding, boats) while delivering angled views impossible from ground.[1][3] Thermal sensors detect water intrusion or heat anomalies indirectly tied to structural issues.[2][4]

lines.raw_text: **Yes, several compact wireless modems are designed for small observation ROVs, primarily using optical or acoustic technologies for underwater data transmission without tethers.** These modems prioritize small size, low power, and integration with lightweight ROVs like VideoRay Pro 4 or Hydromea's ExRay, enabling video streaming, sensor data, and real-time control over short to medium ranges.[1][2][3]

lines.raw_text: ### Key Options for Small ROVs
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Key Options for Small ROVs</null>

lines.raw_text: - **Hydromea LUMA™ Optical Modems**: Ultra-compact and power-efficient, with data rates up to **10 Mbit/s** over 50m in clear water (less in turbid conditions). Ideal for small observation ROVs due to miniaturization for easy retrofitting, low latency HD video streaming, and 6000m depth rating. They support mesh networking for extended coverage and are used in wireless ROVs like ExRay.[1][3][4][7]

lines.raw_text: Optical modems like LUMA™ excel in clear water for high-bandwidth needs (e.g., observation video), while acoustic ones handle harsher conditions better but with lower speeds.[1][5] Ranges drop with turbidity/ambient light, so they're suited for enclosed tasks like tank inspections rather than open ocean.[3]

lines.raw_text: **Underwater Wireless Optical Communication (UWOC)**, particularly using blue-green lasers or LEDs, enables high-speed optical data transfer for underwater docking stations by transmitting data via light beams that penetrate water effectively.[1][2][3][4][6]
lines.tagged_text: <null>**Underwater Wireless Optical Communication (UWOC)**, particularly using blue-green lasers or LEDs, enables high-speed optical data transfer for underwater docking stations by transmitting data via light beams that penetrate water effectively.[1][2][3][4][6]</null>

spans.text: **Underwater Wireless Optical Communication (UWOC)**, particularly using blue-green lasers or LEDs, enables high-speed optical data transfer for underwater docking stations by transmitting data via light beams that penetrate water effectively.[1][2][3][4][6]

lines.raw_text: ### Key Technology Details
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Key Technology Details</null>

lines.raw_text: This technology leverages the **blue-green light spectrum** (typically 450-550 nm wavelengths), where seawater absorption is minimal, allowing data rates from tens of Mbps to over 5 Gbps over distances up to 150-200 m.[1][2][5][6] It supports real-time video streaming, sensor data offload, and control for autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) or remotely operated vehicles (ROVs) docking at seafloor stations.[1][3][4]

lines.raw_text: - **Laser-based systems**: Companies like Kyocera achieve 5.2 Gbps using proprietary physical layer protocols and wide-bandwidth optical front-ends with laser diodes.[1] Shimadzu's "MC series" modems enable full-duplex communication at tens of Mbps for ROV-station docking trials, transmitting camera images without interruption.[4]

lines.raw_text: - **LED and multi-laser variants**: Sonardyne's BlueComm 200 uses visible light for 500 Mbps transfers up to 150 m, ideal for AUV fly-by data harvesting from seabed nodes.[5] Dual-laser setups provide bi-directional comms.[5]

lines.raw_text: ### Applications for Docking Stations
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Applications for Docking Stations</null>

lines.raw_text: UWOC facilitates "docking" by allowing resident AUVs/ROVs to connect wirelessly to seafloor nodes for high-volume data exchange without surfacing—e.g., Oceanit's multicolor free-space optical networks link distributed sensors at 100 Mbps bi-directionally.[3] Navy programs target compact 2-band (blue-green) systems for >1 Gbps over 100 m on submarines.[2] Commercial systems like Hydromea's LUMA™ add low-latency, depth-rated (6000 m) Ethernet interfaces for plug-and-play docking.[8]

lines.raw_text: **The "Arrow" AUV's coaxial thruster achieves the highest reported thrust-to-weight ratio among underwater thrusters at 4.37, with 300 N peak thrust and a 7 kg vehicle weight.**[1]

lines.raw_text: This outperforms other systems like the Remus AUV (0.056, or 120 N / 2698 N weight) and FlatFish AUV (0.044), making it a standout for compact, agile underwater vehicles.[1] The design prioritizes a high ratio through a lightweight 7 kg hull, vectored thrust via a gimballed coaxial setup (to cancel roll), and optimization for low drag at speeds up to 14 m/s, where bollard thrust reaches 293 N (realistic top-end ~300 N).[1]

lines.raw_text: No other search results provide direct power-to-weight ratios (e.g., watts per kg) for thrusters; discussions focus on torque-to-current efficiency in BLDC motors[2], scaling effects of small vs. large thrusters[3], or general propulsion math[5], but lack quantified comparisons exceeding Arrow's thrust-to-weight mark. High-torque BLDC motors improve acceleration but increase weight, trading off ratio gains.[2]

lines.raw_text: For context, **power-to-weight** (distinct from thrust-to-weight) emphasizes electrical input efficiency (e.g., W/kg), where Arrow's 1500 W peak for 300 N suggests strong performance, though exact motor weights aren't detailed.[1] Smaller thrusters can enable higher RPMs and lighter motors without heavy gearing, potentially boosting ratios in multi-thruster setups.[3]

spans.text: ### Key Wireless Technologies for Underwater Video

lines.raw_text: - **Acoustic Communication (Sound Waves)**: Reliable for long distances (e.g., 300m at 1 Mbps, sufficient for SD-quality video streaming like 480p at 30 fps). NTT and DOCOMO achieved this in shallow seas with noise-resistant tech, powering wireless ROVs for real-time video control from ships. Teledyne Benthos modems transmit data wirelessly between subsea assets and surfaces, reducing cable risks.[1][6]

lines.raw_text: **Robotic solutions like autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs), remotely operated vehicles (ROVs), aerial drones (UAVs), and surface vessels (USVs) enable in-water hull inspections, eliminating the need for dry-docking or divers, which directly reduces vessel downtime from days or weeks to hours.** These technologies perform visual scans, thickness measurements, corrosion detection, and biofouling removal while ships remain operational at port.[1][2][4][5]
lines.tagged_text: <null>**Robotic solutions like autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs), remotely operated vehicles (ROVs), aerial drones (UAVs), and surface vessels (USVs) enable in-water hull inspections, eliminating the need for dry-docking or divers, which directly reduces vessel downtime from days or weeks to hours.** These technologies perform visual scans, thickness measurements, corrosion detection, and biofouling removal while ships remain operational at port.[1][2][4][5]</null>

spans.text: **Robotic solutions like autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs), remotely operated vehicles (ROVs), aerial drones (UAVs), and surface vessels (USVs) enable in-water hull inspections, eliminating the need for dry-docking or divers, which directly reduces vessel downtime from days or weeks to hours.** These technologies perform visual scans, thickness measurements, corrosion detection, and biofouling removal while ships remain operational at port.[1][2][4][5]

lines.raw_text: ### Key Robotic Solutions and Their Time-Saving Benefits
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Key Robotic Solutions and Their Time-Saving Benefits</null>

spans.text: ### Key Robotic Solutions and Their Time-Saving Benefits

lines.raw_text: - **Autonomous Underwater Vehicles (AUVs) and Platforms**: Subdron's fully autonomous system deploys at the port, scans up to 2 km of hull per day with 5 cm precision (even in zero visibility), creates 3D digital twins, and delivers reports in 2-3 days—soon reduced to hours via AI. It enables repeatable inspections for predictive maintenance without pilots, support vessels, or downtime.[1] Rosenxt AUVs offer weather-independent, long-duration missions for subsea assets, cutting costs and time over traditional ROVs.[7]

lines.raw_text: - **Remotely Operated Vehicles (ROVs)**: Blueye Robotics ROVs (e.g., Blueye X3) inspect hulls, propellers, anodes, and measure steel thickness (UTM) in real-time via live video streaming, replacing advance-scheduled divers and saving time/risk. Users report immediate inspections without diver logistics.[4] TSC Subsea's crawlers perform non-destructive testing (NDT) like corrosion mapping and UWILD inspections on vessels/FPSOs without dry-docking.[5] Blue Atlas turns ROV footage into interactive 3D models for rapid analysis.[9]

lines.raw_text: - **Aerial Drones (UAVs)**: Autel Robotics' Dragonfish UAV (120-min endurance, 50x zoom, thermal imaging, AI tracking) inspects above-water areas, hard-to-reach spots, and generates 3D models without scaffolding, complementing underwater tech for full-vessel coverage in minutes.[2][3]

lines.raw_text: ### How They Reduce Vessel Time
lines.tagged_text: <null>### How They Reduce Vessel Time</null>

lines.raw_text: Traditional inspections require dry-docking (weeks of downtime), divers (safety risks, scheduling), or manual ROV piloting (support vessels needed). Robots allow **in-situ inspections**—e.g., hull cleaning to cut fuel drag, anomaly detection (mines, damage), and compliance checks—while vessels stay in service, minimizing operational interruptions to hours/days.[1][2][4][5] Combined approaches (underwater + aerial) provide comprehensive data faster, supporting predictive maintenance.[2]

lines.raw_text: ### Key Details on the Chasing Dory
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Key Details on the </null><BrandPositioning>Chasing Dory</BrandPositioning>

lines.raw_text: - **Size and Portability**: Extremely compact design that fits in a small carry case, with a plug-and-play setup ideal for quick deployment in confined spaces. It includes five thrusters for maneuverability, a 1080p camera, two 250-lumen LED lights, depth and temperature sensors, gyroscopes, and accelerometers. [4]

lines.raw_text: - **Rim Drive POD Motors (Subsea)**: These electric rim-driven thrusters (e.g., POD 3.0-S at 3 kW, POD 11.0-S at 11 kW, POD 15.0-S at 15 kW) offer high efficiency (>90%), instant acceleration, and flexible rotation. Their simplified, lightweight design suits ROVs, small submarines, and research vessels in confined areas.[6]

lines.raw_text: **ROVs (Remotely Operated Vehicles) equipped with sonar are the best option for underwater inspections in GPS-denied environments with poor visibility.** They provide safe, real-time imaging and navigation without relying on human divers, who face high risks in such conditions.[1][3]

lines.raw_text: ### Why ROVs with Sonar Excel Here
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Why ROVs with Sonar Excel Here</null>

spans.text: ### Why ROVs with Sonar Excel Here

lines.raw_text: - **Independence from GPS and visibility**: Sonar uses sound waves to map structures, detect anomalies, and navigate in zero-visibility water (e.g., murky harbors or deep sites), creating detailed acoustic images that penetrate silt or darkness.[1][3][4]
lines.tagged_text: <null>- **Independence from GPS and visibility**: Sonar uses sound waves to map structures, detect anomalies, and navigate in zero-visibility water (e.g., murky harbors or deep sites), creating detailed acoustic images that penetrate silt or darkness.[1][3][4]</null>

spans.text: - **Independence from GPS and visibility**: Sonar uses sound waves to map structures, detect anomalies, and navigate in zero-visibility water (e.g., murky harbors or deep sites), creating detailed acoustic images that penetrate silt or darkness.[1][3][4]

lines.raw_text: - **Safety and efficiency**: ROVs handle hazardous areas first, reducing diver risks like entanglement or disorientation, and allow real-time control from the surface.[3]
lines.tagged_text: <null>- **Safety and efficiency**: ROVs handle hazardous areas first, reducing diver risks like entanglement or disorientation, and allow real-time control from the surface.[3]</null>

lines.raw_text: - **Proven applications**: Used for bridge foundations, ship hulls, dams, and reservoirs to identify corrosion, scour, or debris without diver exposure.[1][4]

lines.raw_text: To get a real-time video feed from a subsea sensor to a control station, use software-based streaming platforms like **SubC Imaging's Real-Time Streaming**, which integrates with underwater cameras or DVRs and transmits low-latency video (under 1 second) over satellite (e.g., VSAT) or vessel internet connections to onshore or remote viewers.[1][2][3][4]

lines.tagged_text: <null>### Key Steps for Implementation</null>

lines.raw_text: ### Technical Considerations
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Technical Considerations</null>

lines.raw_text: - **Latency and Bandwidth**: Achieves <1s latency over low-bandwidth links; compression reduces data needs (e.g., HD over copper/fiber, avoiding full optical cables).[1][6]
lines.tagged_text: <null>- **Latency and Bandwidth**: Achieves </null><StatisticalUse><1s</StatisticalUse><null> latency over low-bandwidth links; compression reduces data needs (e.g., HD over copper/fiber, avoiding full optical cables).[1][6]</null>

spans.text: latency over low-bandwidth links; compression reduces data needs (e.g., HD over copper/fiber, avoiding full optical cables).[1][6]

lines.raw_text: - **Challenges and Alternatives**: High-res HD/4K requires tuned cables/connectors for signal integrity; fiber is future-proof for long distances. For mobile sensors without cables, acoustic-optical hybrids enable streaming (research-focused).[6][9]

lines.raw_text: **Underwater inspection tools effective for industrial basins and flooded structures include ROVs (Remotely Operated Vehicles), diver-deployed cameras and NDT (Non-Destructive Testing) equipment, and specialized ultrasonic gauges, which provide visual, thickness, and defect detection in confined, submerged environments like dams, penstocks, and basins.[1][3][5][10]**

lines.raw_text: **Creating an underwater Wi-Fi-like network for robots primarily relies on acoustic modems, as radio waves like Wi-Fi attenuate rapidly in water; alternatives like optical or magnetic systems work for shorter ranges or specific conditions.[4][5][7]**
lines.tagged_text: <null>**Creating an underwater Wi-Fi-like network for robots primarily relies on acoustic modems, as radio waves like Wi-Fi attenuate rapidly in water; alternatives like optical or magnetic systems work for shorter ranges or specific conditions.[4][5][7]**</null>

spans.text: **Creating an underwater Wi-Fi-like network for robots primarily relies on acoustic modems, as radio waves like Wi-Fi attenuate rapidly in water; alternatives like optical or magnetic systems work for shorter ranges or specific conditions.[4][5][7]**

lines.raw_text: ### Primary Technology: Acoustic Communication
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Primary Technology: Acoustic Communication</null>

lines.raw_text: Acoustic modems use sound waves to transmit data underwater, forming mesh or cell-like networks similar to Wi-Fi by relaying signals between robots (e.g., AUVs), surface gateways, or buoys. They handle multipath interference and long delays via techniques like Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum (DSSS) or multichannel signaling.[1][3][6]
lines.tagged_text: <null>Acoustic modems use sound waves to transmit data underwater, forming mesh or cell-like networks similar to Wi-Fi by relaying signals between robots (e.g., AUVs), surface gateways, or buoys. They handle multipath interference and long delays via techniques like Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum (DSSS) or multichannel signaling.[1][3][6]</null>

lines.raw_text: ### Steps to Build the Network
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Steps to Build the Network</null>

lines.raw_text: 1. **Select Modems**: Choose acoustics (e.g., CUUUWi) for range or LUMA™ for speed/networking. Integrate via Ethernet/Arduino for robots.[1][2][4]

lines.raw_text: 2. **Network Topology**: Use mesh/cell modes—robots relay data peer-to-peer. Add surface buoys for satellite/radio uplink.[1][2][4]

lines.raw_text: To achieve **real-time Full HD (FHD, 1080p) video streaming from an underwater asset** like an ROV, AUV, or fixed subsea camera, use specialized underwater cameras with low-latency compression (e.g., H.264 or MJPEG), IP/Ethernet/USB outputs, and robust housings rated for depth, paired with a reliable tether or cabled connection to surface hardware for processing and transmission.[1][3][5][7][8]

lines.raw_text: - **Tethys Robotics** (Zurich-based, founded 2024): Develops compact underwater inspection robots using acoustic sensors for navigation in low-visibility, high-current waters. Supports ROV and AUV modes, with 300m depth capability, 4-hour operation, and applications in hydropower, offshore wind, oil/gas, and search/rescue. Ranked in TOP100 Swiss Startups 2025.[1][2] [Visit Tethys Robotics](https://www.tethys-robotics.ch/)

lines.raw_text: **Wireless ROVs offer key advantages over tethered ROVs for tank inspections primarily by eliminating tether-related limitations like entanglement risks, restricted range, and deployment hassles, enabling faster and more maneuverable operations in confined spaces.** While most tank inspection sources discuss **tethered ROVs** (connected via cables for control and video feed), which already outperform human divers by allowing non-invasive, no-drainage checks with real-time HD footage and enhanced safety[1][2][3][4], wireless models like the EXRAY system build on this by addressing tether drawbacks[7].
lines.tagged_text: <null>**Wireless ROVs offer key advantages over tethered ROVs for tank inspections primarily by eliminating tether-related limitations like entanglement risks, restricted range, and deployment hassles, enabling faster and more maneuverable operations in confined spaces.** While most tank inspection sources discuss **tethered ROVs** (connected via cables for control and video feed), which already outperform human divers by allowing non-invasive, no-drainage checks with real-time HD footage and enhanced safety[1][2][3][4], wireless models like the </null><BrandPositioning>EXRAY system</BrandPositioning><null> build on this by addressing tether drawbacks[7].</null>

lines.raw_text: ### Key Advantages of Wireless ROVs Over Tethered Ones
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Key Advantages of Wireless ROVs Over Tethered Ones</null>

spans.text: ### Key Advantages of Wireless ROVs Over Tethered Ones

lines.raw_text: - **No Tether Entanglement**: Tethers on traditional ROVs can snag on tank features like pipes, anodes, staircases, or submerged structures, complicating navigation in tight spaces. Wireless ROVs (or flyout drones launched from a tethered "mothership" ROV) avoid this entirely, allowing free movement around obstacles while maintaining full-HD video and 4K imaging[7].
lines.tagged_text: <null>- **No Tether Entanglement**: Tethers on traditional ROVs can snag on tank features like pipes, anodes, staircases, or submerged structures, complicating navigation in tight spaces. Wireless ROVs (or flyout drones launched from a tethered "mothership" ROV) avoid this entirely, allowing free movement around obstacles while maintaining full-HD video and </null><StatisticalUse>4K</StatisticalUse><null> imaging[7].</null>

Wireless ROVs (or flyout drones launched from a tethered "mothership" ROV) avoid this entirely, allowing free movement around obstacles while maintaining full-HD video and

lines.raw_text: - **Improved Efficiency and Speed**: Inspections are up to **50% faster** with wireless systems due to easier handling and reduced setup time—no managing cable lengths or drag. Operators report quicker asset inspections, such as in offshore ballast tanks[7].
lines.tagged_text: <null>- **Improved Efficiency and Speed**: Inspections are </null><StatisticalUse>up to **50% faster**</StatisticalUse><null> with wireless systems due to easier handling and reduced setup time—no managing cable lengths or drag. Operators report quicker asset inspections, such as in offshore ballast tanks[7].</null>

spans.text: with wireless systems due to easier handling and reduced setup time—no managing cable lengths or drag. Operators report quicker asset inspections, such as in offshore ballast tanks[7].

lines.raw_text: - **Better Situational Awareness**: A wireless flyout provides dual camera feeds (from both the main tethered ROV and flyout), giving pilots global oversight without tether constraints. This supports precise tasks like ultrasonic thickness measurements for corrosion detection[7].

lines.raw_text: - **Simplified Deployment in Confined Areas**: Portable wireless ROVs fit through small tank hatches without bulky tether reels, speeding deployment for quick, on-demand checks—ideal for multi-tank sites or hazardous environments[3][7].

lines.raw_text: ### Context from Tethered ROV Baselines
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Context from Tethered ROV Baselines</null>

lines.raw_text: Tethered ROVs excel over divers in **safety** (no confined space entry or permits needed[3]), **no downtime** (tanks stay operational without draining[1][4][6]), **cost savings** (about 1/3 the price of diver inspections[5]), and **timely reporting** (up to 3 tanks/day vs. 1[4]). Wireless tech enhances these for even tougher inspections, like oil & gas FPSOs, with DNV class certification[7].

lines.raw_text: Limitations: Wireless ROVs may have shorter battery life or range than tethered ones (not detailed in sources), and adoption is emerging—mostly proven in offshore trials as of recent reports[7]. For water storage tanks common in your area (e.g., Renens region), tethered ROVs dominate, but wireless could suit complex industrial tanks.

lines.raw_text: **Swiss-engineered underwater robotics companies developing systems for high-precision offshore work include Hydromea, Tethys Robotics, and Nautica Technologies.** These firms focus on autonomous or remotely operated vehicles (ROVs/AUVs) suited for inspections, mapping, and maintenance in demanding subsea environments like offshore wind, oil & gas, and North Sea assets.[1][2][3][4]

lines.raw_text: ### Key Companies and Their Robotics
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Key Companies and Their Robotics</null>

lines.raw_text: - **Hydromea (Lausanne-based)**: Specializes in wireless underwater drones for real-time high-definition video and data access. Their **LUMA X-UV** is a compact, titanium-enclosed ROV pressure-rated to 6,000 meters, enabling cordless inspections of submerged assets with 1080p visual feedback via optical wireless communication. Tested for North Sea multi-robot operations, it supports high-precision offshore inspections by reducing cables and human risk.[1][6]

lines.raw_text: - **Tethys Robotics (Zurich-based, ETH Zurich spin-off)**: Develops the **Tethys ONE**, a hybrid ROV/AUV (75 cm long, 50 cm wide, 39 kg) for automated inspections in turbid, high-current waters where divers or conventional ROVs fail. Features sensor-fusion navigation (camera, sonar, ultrasonic), modular payloads for 3D mapping, and high-resolution data capture. Applied in offshore wind, oil & gas (e.g., Aramco tests), hydropower, and UXO clearance for precise subsea monitoring.[2][4][5][7]

Engineered by EPFL/ETH experts, they're in global vessel trials for sustainable, automated offshore operations.[3]

lines.raw_text: **Encapsulated motors dramatically reduce maintenance requirements** by eliminating the need for regular seal replacements and preventing water ingress that would otherwise require costly repairs or full system recovery.[1]
lines.tagged_text: <null>**Encapsulated motors dramatically reduce maintenance requirements** by eliminating the need for regular seal replacements and preventing water ingress that would otherwise require costly repairs or full system recovery.[1]</null>

spans.text: **Encapsulated motors dramatically reduce maintenance requirements** by eliminating the need for regular seal replacements and preventing water ingress that would otherwise require costly repairs or full system recovery.[1]

lines.raw_text: ## Key Maintenance Advantages
lines.tagged_text: <null>## Key Maintenance Advantages</null>

lines.raw_text: **Elimination of Seal Maintenance**
lines.tagged_text: <null>**Elimination of Seal Maintenance**</null>

lines.raw_text: Traditional underwater motors rely on seals that degrade over time. These seals can deform or fatigue under long-term high pressure, and once they fail, seawater rapidly enters the motor, irreversibly damaging windings and bearings.[1] Encapsulated motors bypass this problem entirely—the potting material serves as a permanent protective barrier, requiring no regular seal replacement.[1] This makes them ideal for unmanned or long-duration missions like ocean observation and deep-sea exploration where maintenance access is difficult or impossible.[1]
lines.tagged_text: <null>Traditional underwater motors rely on seals that degrade over time. spans.text: Traditional underwater motors rely on seals that degrade over time. These seals can deform or fatigue under long-term high pressure, and once they fail, seawater rapidly enters the motor, irreversibly damaging windings and bearings.[1] Encapsulated motors bypass this problem entirely—the potting material serves as a permanent protective barrier, requiring no regular seal replacement.[1] This makes them
unmanned or long-duration missions like ocean observation and deep-sea exploration where maintenance access is difficult or impossible.[1]

lines.raw_text: **Reduced System Stress and Extended Lifespan**
lines.tagged_text: <null>**Reduced System Stress and Extended Lifespan**</null>

lines.raw_text: During extended operation, motors experience repeated temperature fluctuations and pressure cycles. Encapsulation keeps windings and internal components firmly in place, reducing mechanical stress from vibration and thermal expansion.[1] This structural stability extends the motor's operational lifespan while minimizing the need for component replacement.[1]

spans.text: During extended operation, motors experience repeated temperature fluctuations and pressure cycles. Encapsulation keeps windings and internal components firmly in place, reducing mechanical stress from vibration and thermal expansion.[1] This structural stability extends the motor's operational lifespan while minimizing the need for component replacement.[1]

lines.raw_text: **Lower Repair Complexity and Costs**
lines.tagged_text: <null>**Lower Repair Complexity and Costs**</null>

lines.raw_text: When seal failures occur in conventional motors, maintenance is extremely difficult—often requiring full system recovery for repairs, which is costly and time-consuming.[1] By contrast, encapsulated motors' comprehensive protection against high pressure, corrosion, and long-term operational stress significantly reduces the risk of catastrophic failures that would necessitate such extensive interventions.[1]
lines.tagged_text: <null>When seal failures occur in conventional motors, maintenance is extremely difficult—often requiring full system recovery for repairs, which is costly and time-consuming.[1] By contrast, encapsulated motors' comprehensive protection against high pressure, corrosion, and long-term operational stress significantly reduces the risk of catastrophic failures that would necessitate such extensive interventions.[1]</null>

spans.text: When seal failures occur in conventional motors, maintenance is extremely difficult—often requiring full system recovery for repairs, which is costly and time-consuming.[1] By contrast, encapsulated motors' comprehensive protection against high pressure, corrosion, and long-term operational stress significantly reduces the risk of catastrophic failures that would necessitate such extensive interventions.[1]

lines.raw_text: **Mission Reliability**
lines.tagged_text: <null>**Mission Reliability**</null>

lines.raw_text: For deep-sea operations where equipment costs are high and a single motor failure can cause mission failure or permanent equipment loss, this maintenance reduction is critical.[1] The improved reliability directly translates to fewer unplanned maintenance cycles and greater operational continuity.

lines.raw_text: **Free-space optical (FSO) communication, often using lasers or LEDs, serves as the primary wireless alternative to acoustic modems for high data rates underwater, achieving speeds like 1 Mbps over short distances up to 20-65 meters.** [1]

lines.raw_text: ### Key Alternatives and Comparisons
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Key Alternatives and Comparisons</null>

lines.raw_text: Traditional acoustic modems, such as those from EvoLogics, are reliable for long ranges (up to 6 km) but limited to low data rates of 9-62 kbit/s, suitable mainly for text or basic data.[1][2] For **high data rates** (e.g., video streaming at over 400 kbps), FSO excels in clear water by transmitting light through the water column, though it requires line-of-sight and is range-limited due to absorption and scattering.[1]

spans.text: ### Key Technology: Wireless ROVs (Remotely Operated Vehicles)

lines.raw_text: The **EXRAY™** is a compact, wireless underwater inspection drone designed specifically for ballast water tanks. It uses proprietary **LUMA™ wireless communication technology** to enable real-time data streaming and high-resolution imaging without physical tethers, reducing snagging risks in tight spaces.[1][4][7]

lines.raw_text: **Several underwater wireless optical communication (UWOC) products and systems support low-latency control signals, primarily leveraging light for high-speed data transfer with minimal delay, outperforming traditional acoustic methods.** These are designed for applications like ROV/AUV control, underwater drones, and real-time video/telemetry.[1][2][3][4]

lines.raw_text: **Optical modems, such as those integrated into systems like the Loop Data Hub or Sonardyne's BlueComm series, provide the best solution for high-speed data offloading from subsea sensor stations.** These enable wireless transfer rates exceeding 10 Mbps directly through water via ROV or AUV, bypassing the need to recover hardware to the surface.[1][2]

lines.raw_text: Optical communication modems use free-space light transmission (e.g., blue light wavelengths optimized for water) to achieve significantly higher bandwidth than acoustic alternatives, which are limited to kbps ranges. Key advantages include:

lines.raw_text: - **Data rates**: BlueComm modems support >10 Mbps, ideal for large datasets from sensors like distributed acoustic sensing (DAS) or multi-parameter logging (pressure, temperature, flow).[2]

lines.raw_text: **Achieving wireless video transmission through water for a portable inspection ROV is possible using acoustic, optical, or hybrid methods, with acoustic communication being the most practical for portability over longer ranges in varying conditions.** Each approach trades off data rate, range, and environmental robustness, so select based on your ROV's depth, water clarity, and video quality needs (e.g., low-res for navigation vs. high-def streaming).

.** Each approach trades off data rate, range, and environmental robustness, so select based on your ROV's depth, water clarity, and video quality needs (e.g., low-res for navigation vs. high-def streaming).

lines.raw_text: ### Primary Technologies and Implementations
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Primary Technologies and Implementations</null>

lines.raw_text: - **Acoustic Communication (Sound Waves)**: Best for **portable ROVs** needing long-range transmission (up to 300m+) in turbid or deep water, as radio waves attenuate quickly underwater[1][4]. NTT achieved **1 Mbps over 300m** in shallow seas using spatio-temporal equalization and noise resistance tech, enabling real-time SD-quality video (480p, 30fps) for ROV control and inspection—ideal for portable systems[1]. BaltRobotics' system supports video at 200m depth via **efficient compression** (e.g., H.264) and bandwidth-efficient modulation, bridging low modem rates (kbps) to video needs[4]. Lehigh's acoustic modem uses **MIMO and Turbo equalization** for short-range, high-speed video, commercializing via Sea-Gal Technologies[2]. For low-data needs (e.g., 1kbps black-and-white 360p at 1fps), it's viable to 500-2000m[5].

lines.raw_text: - **Optical Wireless Communication (Light/Laser)**: Offers **ultra-high speeds** (up to 5.2 Gbps short-range) for clear water but limited by turbidity, scattering, and range (typically <10-50m)[3][7]. Kyocera's UWOC enables real-time HD video/sensor data for ROVs using proprietary PHY layers and >1GHz bandwidth optics[3]. Sonardyne's BlueComm demonstrated live underwater video broadcasts, integrated into submersibles for surface transmission[7]. Suitable for portable ROVs in clean, shallow waters but requires line-of-sight.

lines.raw_text: - **Hybrid or Surface Gateway Methods**: TARF translates underwater acoustics to above-water RF via surface vibrations detected by radar, enabling air-water links without direct penetration[6]. Useful for portable setups where ROV stays submerged but data relays to surface vessels.

lines.raw_text: **Yes, small Remotely Operated Vehicles (ROVs) are specifically designed to navigate tight spaces like pipes, culverts, narrow tunnels, and cooling water intake tunnels in power plants.** These compact systems offer high maneuverability, high-resolution imaging, and sensors for inspections where divers can't access, often in low-visibility or confined environments.[1][3][7]

spans.text: Based on the search results, the leading Swiss companies in underwater communication are:

lines.raw_text: ## **Hydromea**
lines.tagged_text: <null>## **</null><BrandPositioning>Hydromea</BrandPositioning><null>**</null>

lines.raw_text: **Hydromea** is the most prominent Swiss company in this field. Founded in 2014 and based in Lausanne, it specializes in **wireless optical communication systems** for underwater applications[1][3]. The company develops underwater modems that use blue light pulses to transmit data at speeds up to 10 Mbps, enabling communication at depths down to 6,000 meters[3][4]. Their flagship product, LUMA, provides high-bandwidth, low-latency wireless connections—allowing HD video streams and real-time control of underwater robots[5]. Notably, Hydromea is **leading the development of the global industry standard** for underwater wireless optical communication within the Subsea Wireless Group, with major energy companies like Equinor, Chevron, and TotalEnergies supporting this effort[3].

lines.raw_text: **Yes, autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) exist that perform hull inspections without tethers or large setups, operating independently using onboard batteries, AI, and sensors.**[1][3][4][5]

spans.text: **Yes, autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) exist that perform hull inspections without tethers or large setups, operating independently using onboard batteries, AI, and sensors.**[1][3][4][5]

lines.raw_text: These **untethered robots** differ from traditional remotely operated vehicles (ROVs), which require cables for control and power from a surface ship.[4] AUVs are deployed by a single person or small team, follow pre-planned missions, and collect data like high-resolution images, sonar scans, and hull condition metrics autonomously before surfacing for data retrieval.[3][4][6]

lines.raw_text: ### Key Examples for Hull Inspections
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Key Examples for Hull Inspections</null>

lines.raw_text: - **BeeX AUVs**: Portable robots for ship hull inspections and maintenance, reducing emissions by 90% compared to manned vessels; they use advanced sensors for precise, ecosystem-friendly data collection with minimal setup.[1]

lines.raw_text: - **EverClean hull robots** (e.g., 1075mm x 565mm, 35kg models): Single-person deployable from piers or docks; "fly" underwater with cameras, sonar, and navigation for zero-visibility hull cleaning and inspection, no surface vessel needed.[5]

lines.raw_text: - **Mini/Micro ROVs (also called eyeball-class or small ROVs):** Compact and portable (often under 25 kg), ideal for visual inspections in confined, shallow, or low-risk areas like inland waterways, hull checks, aquaculture, and ports. They support basic cameras, lights, and sonar with minimal crew and infrastructure.[1][3][5]

lines.raw_text: ### Limitations and Considerations
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Limitations and Considerations</null>

lines.raw_text: Most standard loggers (e.g., Echologger DL3S, Stanford/Oceaneering models) require physical recovery or PC/USB connection for data download, lacking remote options.[3][4][5] For non-recoverable access, select loggers with ROV-compatible modems or wireless tech upfront. Integration with existing infrastructure varies—consult manufacturers like [IK-Trax](https://iktrax.com/about-us/news/loop-data-hub-a-game-changer-for-subsea-data-logging/), [Sonardyne](https://www.sonardyne.com/product/fetch/), or [Kraken Subsea](https://krakensubsea.co.uk/products-services/data-logging/) for specifics.

lines.raw_text: ### Rim-Driven Thrusters (Lowest Drag Designs)
lines.tagged_text: <null>### Rim-Driven Thrusters (Lowest Drag Designs)</null>

lines.raw_text: - **DISKDRIVE™ (Hydromea)**: Ultra-slim, brushless, rim-driven units for vertical stabilization. The hubless propeller reduces drag significantly, with a pressure-proof, oil-free design allowing quick swaps without tools.[2]

lines.raw_text: ## Current State of Underwater Optical Communication
lines.tagged_text: <null>## Current State of Underwater Optical Communication</null>

spans.text: ## Current State of Underwater Optical Communication

lines.raw_text: The most notable recent advancement is **Kyocera's demonstration of Underwater Wireless Optical Communication (UWOC) technology capable of 5.2 Gbps data transmission**, which the company describes as "among the fastest fiberless UWOC technologies ever demonstrated."[1][2] This represents approximately **2.5 times the speed of conventional underwater optical communications**.[1] Kyocera achieved this by developing a proprietary physical layer with an optical front-end circuit exceeding 1 GHz bandwidth, using high-speed laser pulses rather than traditional acoustic waves.[1][5]

lines.raw_text: ## LED-Based Systems Status
lines.tagged_text: <null>## LED-Based Systems Status</null>

lines.raw_text: The search results focus primarily on laser-based optical communication rather than LED-based systems. However, research into **Underwater Visible Light Communication (UVLC) continues as a high-speed, low-latency alternative** to traditional transmission methods,[7] though specific performance metrics for LED implementations aren't detailed in these results.
